Frettly

This document aggregates the public documentation from frettly.app for
AI assistants.

Frettly – Guitar Scales & Chords Companion

Frettly - the interactive fretboard explorer and notebook

Frettly is a macOS application dedicated to enabling players of fretted
instruments to visualise, experiment, and explore scales, modes and
chords using a document-based canvas.

Think of Frettly as Pages, not for text but for fretboards.

Frettly isn’t just a scale and chord library - it’s a persistent,
document-based workspace designed by a player, for players. Whether you
are composing a complex jazz piece, teaching a student, or exploring a
new tuning on your banjo, Frettly organises your theory explorations
into easily read, playable, shareable and saveable documents.

Explore scales, modes and chords in a persistent, document-based macOS
workspace.

Supporting guitar, bass guitar, mandolin, banjo and ukulele , each document you create can show multiple scales, chords and modes for the document’s chosen instrument, and each instrument type supports multiple possible tunings.

The roadmap for future Frettly enhancements is brimming with features.
If you have suggestions for features or improvements, need support or
advice, contact us at:

hello@frettly.app

What you can do with Frettly

-   Build visual music documents from fretboards, scales, modes, chords
    and chord organisers, all arranged on a flexible document canvas

-   Switch instruments, tunings or handedness at any time and watch
    every fretboard and chord panel adapt to the new context

-   Keep separate documents for lessons, songs, theory explorations,
    practice routines, alternate tunings or ideas you want to return to
    later

-   Listen to notes, scale patterns, modes and strummed chords with
    pitch-perfect, instrument-specific playback as you explore the
    fretboard

-   Focus your learning by showing notes, intervals, marked notes, or
    only the parts of a pattern you want to study right now

-   Highlight important notes and intervals, add your own panel notes,
    and capture teaching points, discoveries, reminders and practice
    ideas

-   Rearrange, duplicate, copy and move musical ideas freely, both
    within a single Frettly document and between different documents

-   Personalise the look of your workspace with fretboard backgrounds
    and note colour themes, making documents clearer and more enjoyable
    to use

Frettly is a product of Efford Studio Limited

Efford Studio Limited is an independent UK software studio focused on
thoughtfully crafted tools for musicians and creatives.

Frettly Pro – One-Time Purchase

Unlock Frettly Pro

Unlock Frettly Pro with a one-time purchase on the Mac App Store.

Explore the complete collection of scales, modes, chords and tunings,
create richly interactive fretboards and chord organisers, and save your
work in reusable Frettly documents.

There are no monthly or annual subscriptions, and Family Sharing is
supported.

Why Go Pro?

Frettly began as a personal project for exploring the guitar through
clear visualisation, practical music theory and interactive sound.

With Frettly Pro, you can combine multiple scale, mode and chord
fretboards within reusable documents, create chord organisers, explore
different tunings and build a personal library that supports your
playing, learning and songwriting.

Your purchase unlocks the complete current feature set while also
supporting the continued development of Frettly.

When you go Pro, you’re getting more than just the current feature set —
you’re also supporting ongoing development and helping to shape where
Frettly goes next.

What You Get with Frettly Pro

-   Create fretboards using all 14 scale types, 9 modes and 44 chord
    types currently included with Frettly.

-   Use the complete collection of instrument tunings.

-   Create and save documents containing multiple fretboards and chord
    organisers.

-   Explore patterns interactively with note marking, interval display
    and audio playback.

-   Receive bug fixes, performance improvements and refinements to
    existing features at no additional cost.

-   Benefit from future additions to the existing scale, mode, chord and
    tuning collections.

There are no subscriptions — just a straightforward, one-time purchase
on the Mac App Store.

Occasionally, substantial new modules may be available separately. If
that happens, they’ll be clearly labelled and entirely optional — you’ll
always keep what you’ve already unlocked.

How to Purchase

Frettly is available from the Mac App Store. Download Frettly, then
unlock Frettly Pro from within the app with a one-time purchase.

No monthly or annual subscription is required, and Family Sharing is
enabled.

Download Frettly free from the Mac App Store, then unlock Frettly Pro
with a one-time in-app purchase.

Feedback

If you have suggestions for improvements or new features, we’d love to
hear from you. Feedback from guitarists will help shape future Frettly
improvements.

Email us at: hello@frettly.app

Frettly – Help & Info

Help & Info

This section collects short guides to key parts of Frettly. Each topic
focuses on a specific scenario or feature, with a screenshot showing
what you can expect to see in the app.

-   Getting started Creating your first document and fretboard

-   Editing essentials Pro tips for editing in Frettly

-   Document window overview An overview of the controls within the
    document window

-   Fretboard panels overview An overview of the controls within
    fretboard panels

-   Chord organiser panels overview An overview of the controls within
    chord organiser panels

-   Instruments and tunings An overview of the instruments Frettly
    supports

-   Settings Application wide settings within Frettly

Getting Started with Frettly

Getting started

When you create a new document in Frettly, you’ll be asked to select an
instrument type, its tuning, and whether you are right- or left-handed.

The initial document settings dialog presented on creating a new
document

-   Select an instrument from: Guitar Guitar (Seven String) Banjo Banjo
    (Four String) Bass Guitar Bass Guitar (Five String) Bass Guitar (Six
    String) Mandolin Ukulele

-   Guitar

-   Guitar (Seven String)

-   Banjo

-   Banjo (Four String)

-   Bass Guitar

-   Bass Guitar (Five String)

-   Bass Guitar (Six String)

-   Mandolin

-   Ukulele

-   Select a tuning for that instrument - Frettly comes with multiple
    instrument-specific tunings. Note that in the free version of
    Frettly, the choice of tuning is limited to the standard tuning for
    the selected instrument.

-   By default, all fretboards are for right-handed players, with the
    nut end on the left. Select “Left-handed” in the document’s settings
    and all its fretboards will display with the nut end on the right.

-   The defaulting to right-handed at document creation time can be
    changed to left-handed in menubar Frettly -> Settings, Documents
    tab.

The instrument/tuning/handedness selections made when the document is
created will determine the appearance and layout of all fretboards
created within it, and will be re-applied when the document is reloaded.
The selections made are summarised in the top left of the document
window.

Document title showing instrument and tuning

However, you can change any of these choices later using the controls
icon to the right of the instrument/tuning title at the top of the
document. When you click “Apply”, all fretboards and chord organisers in
the document update to match the new settings. For example: start with
guitar, standard tuning, right-handed — then switch to a left-handed
bass guitar with Drop D tuning, and all panels adjust automatically.

Once you have made that selection and clicked “Apply”, you will be
presented with your new but still empty document. Add a fretboard or
chord organiser panel using the buttons in the new document welcome page
or use the ‘Add Panel’ button in the top right of the document:

Document controls showing “Add Panel”

Selecting “Add Chord Organiser” will simply prompt you for the new
panels title, whereas “Add Fretboard” will open the fretboard config
dialog:

Add fretboard dialog

-   Select your fretboard’s “Content type” from Scale, Mode or Chord.

-   Select the root note (P1) for the chosen content type.

-   Select the pattern to be used for this fretboard.

For the “Scale” content type, the available patterns are:

-   Major

-   Minor

-   Melodic Minor

-   Harmonic Minor

-   Hungarian Major

-   Hungarian Minor

-   Byzantine

-   Major Pentatonic

-   Minor Pentatonic

-   Major Blues

-   Minor Blues

-   Bebop Major

-   Bebop Dominant

-   Whole Tone

-   Diminished (Half-Whole)

-   Diminished (Whole-Half)

-   Chromatic (all 12 notes)

For the “Mode” content type, the available patterns are:

-   Ionian

-   Dorian

-   Dorian♭2

-   Phrygian

-   Phrygian Dominant

-   Lydian

-   Lydian Dominant

-   Lydian Augmented

-   Mixolydian

-   Mixolydian ♭6

-   Aeolian

-   Locrian

-   Locrian

-   Locrian ♯2

-   Altered (Super Locrian)

For the “Chord” content type, the available patterns are:

-   maj

-   m

-   dim

-   aug

-   5

-   sus2

-   sus4

-   add2

-   madd2

-   add9

-   madd9

-   add11

-   6

-   m6

-   6/9

-   m6/9

-   dim6

-   aug6

-   7

-   maj7

-   m7

-   m(maj7)

-   7sus4

-   7sus2

-   m7b5

-   dim7

-   7#5

-   7b5

-   maj7#5

-   9

-   maj9

-   m9

-   9sus4

-   7b9

-   7#9

-   m7(add11)

-   m11

-   11

-   7#11

-   maj7#11

-   7add13

-   13

-   maj13

-   min13

-   13sus4

-   13b9

-   13#11

Once your fretboard is created, you can others or mix them with chord
organiser panels. All the fretboards and chord organisers you create are
stored in the current document. Open it tomorrow and they’ll still be
there waiting for you.

Create a document for individual instruments or tunings, or for multiple
lessons or projects for just one. How you organise your documents is
entirely up to you!

Frettly – Instruments and tunings

Instruments and tunings

Frettly can display scales, chords, and modes — and the notes within
them — across the fretboards of different instrument types, each of
which includes multiple tunings. When you click a note or interval on
the fretboard, Frettly plays the note pitch-perfect, in the octave and
string position you clicked, using instrument-specific samples.

-   Guitar (six-string) Standard (E, A, D, G, B, E) Celtic (D, A, D, G,
    A, D) Dobro (G, B, D, G, B, D) Asus4 (E, A, D, E, A, E) Half-step
    down (Eb, Ab, Db, Gb, Bb, Eb) B modal (B, F#, C#, F#, B, D#) Full
    step down (D, G, C, F, A, D) 1.5 steps down (Db, Gb, B, E, Ab, Db)
    Two steps down (C, F, Bb, Eb, G, C) Baritone (B, E, A, D, F#, B)
    Drop D (D, A, D, G, B, E) Drop C# (C#, G#, C#, F#, A#, D#) Drop C
    (C, G, C, F, A, D) Drop B (B, F#, B, E, G#, C#) Drop A# (A#, F, A#,
    D#, G, C) Drop A (A, E, A, D, F#, B) Drop G# (G#, D#, G#, C#, F, A#)
    Drop E (E, B, E, A, C#, F#) Double drop D (D, A, D, G, B, D) Double
    drop C# (C#, G#, C#, F#, A#, C#) Double drop C (C, G, C, F, A, C)
    Double drop B (B, F#, B, E, G#, B) Double drop A# (Bb, F, Bb, Eb, G,
    Bb) Double drop A (A, E, A, D, F#, A) Cross note C (C, G, C, G, C,
    Eb) Cross note D (D, A, D, F, A, D) Cross note E (E, B, E, G, B, E)
    Cross note F (F, Ab, C, F, C, F) Cross note G (D, G, D, G, Bb, D)
    Cross note A (E, A, E, A, C, E) Open A (E, A, C#, E, A, E) Open B
    (B, F#, B, F#, B, D#) Open C (C, G, C, G, C, E) Open D (D, A, D, F#,
    A, D) Open E (E, B, E, G#, B, E) Open F (F, A, C, F, C, F) Open G
    (D, G, D, G, B, D) Math rock (F, A, C, G, C, E)

Guitar (six-string)

-   Standard (E, A, D, G, B, E)

-   Celtic (D, A, D, G, A, D)

-   Dobro (G, B, D, G, B, D)

-   Asus4 (E, A, D, E, A, E)

-   Half-step down (Eb, Ab, Db, Gb, Bb, Eb)

-   B modal (B, F#, C#, F#, B, D#)

-   Full step down (D, G, C, F, A, D)

-   1.5 steps down (Db, Gb, B, E, Ab, Db)

-   Two steps down (C, F, Bb, Eb, G, C)

-   Baritone (B, E, A, D, F#, B)

-   Drop D (D, A, D, G, B, E)

-   Drop C# (C#, G#, C#, F#, A#, D#)

-   Drop C (C, G, C, F, A, D)

-   Drop B (B, F#, B, E, G#, C#)

-   Drop A# (A#, F, A#, D#, G, C)

-   Drop A (A, E, A, D, F#, B)

-   Drop G# (G#, D#, G#, C#, F, A#)

-   Drop E (E, B, E, A, C#, F#)

-   Double drop D (D, A, D, G, B, D)

-   Double drop C# (C#, G#, C#, F#, A#, C#)

-   Double drop C (C, G, C, F, A, C)

-   Double drop B (B, F#, B, E, G#, B)

-   Double drop A# (Bb, F, Bb, Eb, G, Bb)

-   Double drop A (A, E, A, D, F#, A)

-   Cross note C (C, G, C, G, C, Eb)

-   Cross note D (D, A, D, F, A, D)

-   Cross note E (E, B, E, G, B, E)

-   Cross note F (F, Ab, C, F, C, F)

-   Cross note G (D, G, D, G, Bb, D)

-   Cross note A (E, A, E, A, C, E)

-   Open A (E, A, C#, E, A, E)

-   Open B (B, F#, B, F#, B, D#)

-   Open C (C, G, C, G, C, E)

-   Open D (D, A, D, F#, A, D)

-   Open E (E, B, E, G#, B, E)

-   Open F (F, A, C, F, C, F)

-   Open G (D, G, D, G, B, D)

-   Math rock (F, A, C, G, C, E)

-   Guitar (seven-string) Standard (B, E, A, D, G, B, E) Drop A (A, E,
    A, D, G, B, E) High A (E, A, D, G, B, E, A) A standard (A, D, G, C,
    F, A, D)

Guitar (seven-string)

-   Standard (B, E, A, D, G, B, E)

-   Drop A (A, E, A, D, G, B, E)

-   High A (E, A, D, G, B, E, A)

-   A standard (A, D, G, C, F, A, D)

-   Banjo (five-string) Standard G (G, D, G, B, D) Double C (G, C, G,
    C, D) Sawmill / Mountain minor (G, D, G, C, D) Open D (F#, D, F#,
    A, D) C tuning (G, C, G, B, D)

Banjo (five-string)

-   Standard G (G, D, G, B, D)

-   Double C (G, C, G, C, D)

-   Sawmill / Mountain minor (G, D, G, C, D)

-   Open D (F#, D, F#, A, D)

-   C tuning (G, C, G, B, D)

-   Banjo (four-string) Tenor tuning (C, G, D, A) Irish tuning (G, D,
    A, E) Chicago tuning (D, G, B, E) Plectrum tuning (C, G, B, D)

Banjo (four-string)

-   Tenor tuning (C, G, D, A)

-   Irish tuning (G, D, A, E)

-   Chicago tuning (D, G, B, E)

-   Plectrum tuning (C, G, B, D)

-   Bass guitar (four-string) Standard (E, A, D, G) Half-step down (Eb,
    Ab, Db, Gb) Full step down (D, G, C, F) 1.5 steps down (Db, Gb,
    B, E) Two steps down (C, F, Bb, Eb) Drop D (D, A, D, G) Drop C# (C#,
    G#, C#, F#) Drop C (C, G, C, F) Drop B (B, F#, B, E) Drop A# (Bb, F,
    Bb, Eb) Drop A (A, E, A, D)

Bass guitar (four-string)

-   Standard (E, A, D, G)

-   Half-step down (Eb, Ab, Db, Gb)

-   Full step down (D, G, C, F)

-   1.5 steps down (Db, Gb, B, E)

-   Two steps down (C, F, Bb, Eb)

-   Drop D (D, A, D, G)

-   Drop C# (C#, G#, C#, F#)

-   Drop C (C, G, C, F)

-   Drop B (B, F#, B, E)

-   Drop A# (Bb, F, Bb, Eb)

-   Drop A (A, E, A, D)

-   Bass guitar (five-string) Standard (B, E, A, D, G) High C (E, A, D,
    G, C) Drop A (A, E, A, D, G) Whole step down (A, D, G, C, F)

Bass guitar (five-string)

-   Standard (B, E, A, D, G)

-   High C (E, A, D, G, C)

-   Drop A (A, E, A, D, G)

-   Whole step down (A, D, G, C, F)

-   Mandolin Standard (G, D, A, E) Cross G (G, D, G, D) Old-time (A, D,
    A, E) Fiddle cross (A, E, A, E) Open G (G, D, G, B) Open D (D, A, D,
    F#) Open A (A, E, A, C#) Lowered F (F, C, G, D) Lowered E (E, B, F#,
    C#)

Mandolin

-   Standard (G, D, A, E)

-   Cross G (G, D, G, D)

-   Old-time (A, D, A, E)

-   Fiddle cross (A, E, A, E)

-   Open G (G, D, G, B)

-   Open D (D, A, D, F#)

-   Open A (A, E, A, C#)

-   Lowered F (F, C, G, D)

-   Lowered E (E, B, F#, C#)

-   Ukulele Standard (G, C, E, A) Baritone (D, G, B, E) Open G (G, B,
    D, G) Open A (A, C, E, A) Open D (D, A, D, F#) Open C (C, G, C, E)
    Low A (A, C, E, A) Low G (G, C, E, A) D tuning (A, D, F#, B) Slack
    key D (D, A, D, A)

Ukulele

-   Standard (G, C, E, A)

-   Baritone (D, G, B, E)

-   Open G (G, B, D, G)

-   Open A (A, C, E, A)

-   Open D (D, A, D, F#)

-   Open C (C, G, C, E)

-   Low A (A, C, E, A)

-   Low G (G, C, E, A)

-   D tuning (A, D, F#, B)

-   Slack key D (D, A, D, A)

-   Is your favourite tuning missing? Drop us a line and we’ll add it to
    the next release of Frettly: hello@frettly.app

Is your favourite tuning missing? Drop us a line and we’ll add it to the
next release of Frettly:

Frettly – Document window overview

Document window overview

Frettly is built around the macOS document model — similar to Pages.
Rather than showing a single fretboard that resets each time the app is
opened, Frettly lets you create documents and collect your chosen
fretboards inside them.

These document files become your notebooks for learning, projects, and
discovery: collect the blues scales you want to learn, the minor chords
you want to practise, and so on. Because Frettly uses documents, they
can be moved, duplicated, shared, and even reverted using Time Machine.

If you create two documents — one for guitar and the other for bass —
you can switch between them and Frettly will play notes using each
document’s configured instrument. Don’t forget to explore the macOS menu
bar’s File menu.

-   Document level controls The controls in the top-right of a document
    window affect Frettly’s overall behaviour and display throughout the
    current document, while fretboard controls are shown per fretboard.
    Frettly document-level controls From left to right, the highlighted
    controls are described below.

Document level controls

The controls in the top-right of a document window affect Frettly’s
overall behaviour and display throughout the current document, while
fretboard controls are shown per fretboard.

Frettly document-level controls

From left to right, the highlighted controls are described below.

-   Play mode Frettly has three different ways of playing notes you
    click within fretboards: Sustained play — each clicked note plays
    for a few seconds before fading. Multiple notes can be played in
    quick succession to get a feel for how they sound together, helping
    you hear the relationship between intervals. Single play — click and
    hold to hear a note; on release, the note stops. This mode lets you
    train your ear to a single note or interval without other notes
    ringing. Pattern play — click the root note of a pattern and Frettly
    plays the notes in that fretboard’s pattern along the string. For a
    scale, you will hear seven notes (assuming you have not hidden any —
    see the fretboard help for hiding notes). For a chord, Frettly plays
    the chord tones in sequence (again, assuming none are hidden), then
    finishes with a strum of all notes. So assuming the chord is Emaj
    and you click the E on the nut of string 6, the sequence you hear
    will start with that E, followed by the G# on fret 4 and B on fret
    7, of the same string, followed by a strum of those three together.
    The strum is not intended to represent a specific chord
    voicing/fingering, as no fingering is determined. To hear actual
    voicings strummed, see chord organisers.

Play mode

Frettly has three different ways of playing notes you click within
fretboards:

-   Sustained play — each clicked note plays for a few seconds before
    fading. Multiple notes can be played in quick succession to get a
    feel for how they sound together, helping you hear the relationship
    between intervals.

-   Single play — click and hold to hear a note; on release, the note
    stops. This mode lets you train your ear to a single note or
    interval without other notes ringing.

-   Pattern play — click the root note of a pattern and Frettly plays
    the notes in that fretboard’s pattern along the string. For a scale,
    you will hear seven notes (assuming you have not hidden any — see
    the fretboard help for hiding notes). For a chord, Frettly plays the
    chord tones in sequence (again, assuming none are hidden), then
    finishes with a strum of all notes. So assuming the chord is Emaj
    and you click the E on the nut of string 6, the sequence you hear
    will start with that E, followed by the G# on fret 4 and B on fret
    7, of the same string, followed by a strum of those three together.
    The strum is not intended to represent a specific chord
    voicing/fingering, as no fingering is determined. To hear actual
    voicings strummed, see chord organisers.

-   Jump to current panel When you click the title of a panel, it
    becomes the currently focused one. The Up and Down arrow keys move
    that focus up and down, one panel at a time. Focus is primarily a
    navigational aid: controls (and notes) across all fretboards and
    chord organisers remain usable, regardless of which one is currently
    focused. Focus can be especially useful in documents with many
    panels. For example, you might keep focus on a “primary” scale and
    quickly return to it from anywhere in the document. “Jump to current
    panel” takes you straight back to the last panel you selected.

Jump to current panel

When you click the title of a panel, it becomes the currently focused
one. The Up and Down arrow keys move that focus up and down, one panel
at a time. Focus is primarily a navigational aid: controls (and notes)
across all fretboards and chord organisers remain usable, regardless of
which one is currently focused.

Focus can be especially useful in documents with many panels. For
example, you might keep focus on a “primary” scale and quickly return to
it from anywhere in the document. “Jump to current panel” takes you
straight back to the last panel you selected.

-   Arrange panels The “Arrange panels” button opens a dialog where you
    can manage panel order and navigation. You will notice that in the
    document, each panel has a number in its title area. After moving a
    panel, its number will change as the numbering is always sequential
    within the document. Those numbers show you a panel’s relative
    position in the document. Arrange panels dialog Reorder panels: you
    can rearrange the order of panels in one of two ways within the
    dialog: either drag and drop the row representing a panel, or use
    the Up/Down chevron buttons. The order you choose becomes effective
    when you click “Save”. The list of panels will show the current
    numeric position of each in the document, even after a panel is
    moved. It’s new position does not come into effect until you select
    “Save”. Set focus: each row has a radio button selector. Choose one
    and, when you click “Apply”, that panel becomes the focused panel
    and is brought into view. Preview panels: you can also sample
    fretboard panels quickly using “Pattern play”, without needing to
    scroll to each one in the document. An alternative to using the
    “Arrange panels” dialog to re-arrange panels, is to drag a panel in
    the document using its title area, showing a three bar drag handle
    to remind you. Draggable fretboard title Click and hold anywhere in
    the title area and drag it into its intended position (while
    dragging you should see a snaphot of just the title being dragged).
    A blue highlight line will appear while dragging to show you where
    the dropped panel will be inserted.

Arrange panels

The “Arrange panels” button opens a dialog where you can manage panel
order and navigation. You will notice that in the document, each panel
has a number in its title area. After moving a panel, its number will
change as the numbering is always sequential within the document. Those
numbers show you a panel’s relative position in the document.

Arrange panels dialog

Reorder panels: you can rearrange the order of panels in one of two ways
within the dialog: either drag and drop the row representing a panel, or
use the Up/Down chevron buttons. The order you choose becomes effective
when you click “Save”. The list of panels will show the current numeric
position of each in the document, even after a panel is moved. It’s new
position does not come into effect until you select “Save”.

Set focus: each row has a radio button selector. Choose one and, when
you click “Apply”, that panel becomes the focused panel and is brought
into view.

Preview panels: you can also sample fretboard panels quickly using
“Pattern play”, without needing to scroll to each one in the document.

An alternative to using the “Arrange panels” dialog to re-arrange
panels, is to drag a panel in the document using its title area, showing
a three bar drag handle to remind you.

Draggable fretboard title

Click and hold anywhere in the title area and drag it into its intended
position (while dragging you should see a snaphot of just the title
being dragged). A blue highlight line will appear while dragging to show
you where the dropped panel will be inserted.

-   Add Panel Mentioned in the “Getting started” section of this site,
    the “Add panel” button opens an inline menu offering the choice of
    “Add Fretboard” and “Add Chord Organiser”. When adding a chord
    organiser you will simply be prompted for the title of that new
    panel only, but on adding a fretboard panel you will be presented
    with a dialog where you choose a content type (scale/mode/chord), a
    root note, and a pattern for the new fretboard panel. A fretboard
    panel’s title will always describe those choices and is not
    editable. Add fretboard dialog

Add Panel

Mentioned in the “Getting started” section of this site, the “Add panel”
button opens an inline menu offering the choice of “Add Fretboard” and
“Add Chord Organiser”. When adding a chord organiser you will simply be
prompted for the title of that new panel only, but on adding a fretboard
panel you will be presented with a dialog where you choose a content
type (scale/mode/chord), a root note, and a pattern for the new
fretboard panel. A fretboard panel’s title will always describe those
choices and is not editable.

Add fretboard dialog

-   Help The Help button opens Frettly’s lightweight in-app help dialog.
    The macOS menu bar “Help” → “Frettly Help” opens the content you are
    reading right now in your browser.

Help

The Help button opens Frettly’s lightweight in-app help dialog. The
macOS menu bar “Help” → “Frettly Help” opens the content you are reading
right now in your browser.

Fretboard Panels in Frettly

Fretboard panels overview

Frettly is designed as a macOS document-based application. Rather than
showing a single fretboard that resets the next time the app is opened,
Frettly lets you collect many fretboards within each document.

-   Remember: all scales and chords shown by panels within the same
    document use that document’s current instrument, tuning, and
    handedness. You can create multiple documents, each for a different
    instrument or tuning, or create hundreds of documents all for the
    same instrument.

-   New fretboards or chord organisers are added to the bottom of the
    document. You can reorder them by dragging and dropping a panel
    before or after another, or by using the document’s “Arrange panels”
    sheet.

-   Zoom panels within a document using the standard macOS ⌘+ and ⌘-
    shortcuts, pinch to zoom on the trackpad, menu options under “View”,
    or the zoom controls at the top of each document.

-   If a fretboard extends off-screen horizontally, scroll it left and
    right with your trackpad or mouse to bring hidden sections into
    view.

-   Clicking the title of a panel makes it the currently focused panel
    for navigation. The Up and Down arrow keys move the focus while
    bringing the newly focused panel into view.

-   Fretboard configuration To edit the type, root, or pattern of a
    fretboard, click the configuration icon to the right of the panel
    title to open its edit sheet. Fretboard controls (top left)
    Right-clicking the fretboard title reveals a context menu with two
    extra options. The first duplicates the current fretboard in its
    entirety, including its notepad and marking groups. The second
    deletes the fretboard, which can also be done by clicking the title
    to bring focus to it and pressing the Delete key. Fretboard title
    context menu

Fretboard configuration

To edit the type, root, or pattern of a fretboard, click the
configuration icon to the right of the panel title to open its edit
sheet.

Fretboard controls (top left)

Right-clicking the fretboard title reveals a context menu with two extra
options. The first duplicates the current fretboard in its entirety,
including its notepad and marking groups. The second deletes the
fretboard, which can also be done by clicking the title to bring focus
to it and pressing the Delete key.

Fretboard title context menu

-   Scale Harmony sheet For fretboards with a content type of Scale or
    Mode that supports diatonic chord derivation—that is, heptatonic
    patterns—the button to the right of the configuration icon opens a
    sheet showing the diatonic chords in that key. This single sheet is
    a nexus for useful scale-related actions, as they all pivot on the
    chords derived from a scale. Scale Harmony sheet By default, the
    triad notes are shown for each diatonic chord. Each chord can be
    previewed using the play button to hear it strummed. The voicing
    used is the first resolved voicing that would be shown for that
    chord in a chord organiser. Switch the derived chords to tetrads
    using the “Chord type” control at the top right. All chords derived
    from the scale are shown by default, but the “Progression” picker at
    the top allows you to filter and rearrange the chords using a
    selection of progressions: All chords 3-chord (I-IV-V) Pop
    (I-V-vi-IV) Jazz cadence (ii-V-I) Turnaround (I-vi-ii-V) Jazz
    turnaround (vi-ii-V-I) Circle sequence (iii-vi-ii-V) Pop variant
    (vi-IV-I-V) 50s progression (I-vi-IV-V) Canon (I-V-vi-iii-IV-I-IV-V)
    Minor cadence (I-IV-V) Minor pop (I-vi-iii-vii°) This sheet has a
    help feature which, when selected, slides out a side panel
    explaining how each chord in the list has been derived from its
    parent scale and key. A fretboard panel can be created from each of
    the chords individually. The “Create Chord Organiser” button creates
    a new panel below the parent fretboard panel, containing a chord
    card for each of the chords shown, in the order in which they
    appear. These may be all of the default diatonic chords derived from
    the scale, or only the chords from a selected progression. Each
    fretboard panel has up to nine marking groups, described below,
    which can be used to focus on only the notes that you have marked
    across the fretboard. “Replace Marked Groups” replaces any markings
    you have made in those groups with markings for the notes within
    these chords, and renames each marking group to match its chord.

Scale Harmony sheet

For fretboards with a content type of Scale or Mode that supports
diatonic chord derivation—that is, heptatonic patterns—the button to the
right of the configuration icon opens a sheet showing the diatonic
chords in that key. This single sheet is a nexus for useful
scale-related actions, as they all pivot on the chords derived from a
scale.

Scale Harmony sheet

By default, the triad notes are shown for each diatonic chord. Each
chord can be previewed using the play button to hear it strummed. The
voicing used is the first resolved voicing that would be shown for that
chord in a chord organiser.

Switch the derived chords to tetrads using the “Chord type” control at
the top right.

All chords derived from the scale are shown by default, but the
“Progression” picker at the top allows you to filter and rearrange the
chords using a selection of progressions:

-   All chords

-   3-chord (I-IV-V)

-   Pop (I-V-vi-IV)

-   Jazz cadence (ii-V-I)

-   Turnaround (I-vi-ii-V)

-   Jazz turnaround (vi-ii-V-I)

-   Circle sequence (iii-vi-ii-V)

-   Pop variant (vi-IV-I-V)

-   50s progression (I-vi-IV-V)

-   Canon (I-V-vi-iii-IV-I-IV-V)

-   Minor cadence (I-IV-V)

-   Minor pop (I-vi-iii-vii°)

This sheet has a help feature which, when selected, slides out a side
panel explaining how each chord in the list has been derived from its
parent scale and key.

A fretboard panel can be created from each of the chords individually.

The “Create Chord Organiser” button creates a new panel below the parent
fretboard panel, containing a chord card for each of the chords shown,
in the order in which they appear. These may be all of the default
diatonic chords derived from the scale, or only the chords from a
selected progression.

Each fretboard panel has up to nine marking groups, described below,
which can be used to focus on only the notes that you have marked across
the fretboard. “Replace Marked Groups” replaces any markings you have
made in those groups with markings for the notes within these chords,
and renames each marking group to match its chord.

-   Fretboard string controls To the left of each fretboard, regardless
    of its handedness, are small toggle buttons aligned with the
    strings. These can be used to hide all of the notes on individual
    instrument strings, allowing you to focus your learning on one or
    two strings without being distracted by notes on the other strings.
    Fretboard string controls, defaulted Once one or more strings have
    been hidden, a reset button below the string controls is enabled.
    This resets all strings in the current fretboard to be visible
    again. Alternatively, you can toggle them back on individually.
    Fretboard string controls, after selections Paired with selectively
    hiding notes using the note or interval chips, described in the
    following section, and using the marking groups described later,
    these controls enable you to create customised views of the notes
    across a fretboard.

Fretboard string controls

To the left of each fretboard, regardless of its handedness, are small
toggle buttons aligned with the strings. These can be used to hide all
of the notes on individual instrument strings, allowing you to focus
your learning on one or two strings without being distracted by notes on
the other strings.

Fretboard string controls, defaulted

Once one or more strings have been hidden, a reset button below the
string controls is enabled. This resets all strings in the current
fretboard to be visible again. Alternatively, you can toggle them back
on individually.

Fretboard string controls, after selections

Paired with selectively hiding notes using the note or interval chips,
described in the following section, and using the marking groups
described later, these controls enable you to create customised views of
the notes across a fretboard.

-   Fretboard note/interval chips The notes in each fretboard’s scale,
    mode, or chord are shown as chips at the top of the panel: Fretboard
    note and interval chips Each chip shows a note and its interval
    within the pattern, using the following notations: P1 — Perfect
    unison (root) m2 — Minor second M2 — Major second m3 — Minor third
    M3 — Major third P4 — Perfect fourth d5 — Diminished fifth A4 —
    Augmented fourth P5 — Perfect fifth A5 — Augmented fifth m6 — Minor
    sixth M6 — Major sixth d7 — Diminished seventh m7 — Minor seventh M7
    — Major seventh P8 — Perfect octave m9 — Minor ninth M9 — Major
    ninth m10 — Minor tenth M10 — Major tenth P11 — Perfect eleventh A11
    — Augmented eleventh P12 — Perfect twelfth m13 — Minor thirteenth
    M13 — Major thirteenth Hovering over a chip highlights that note or
    interval across the fretboard. Clicking a chip toggles that note or
    interval between visible and hidden across the fretboard: Example of
    hidden notes Note: all notes can be re-enabled using the reset icon
    to the right of the chips. Note: hidden notes are not played when
    using “Pattern play”.

Fretboard note/interval chips

The notes in each fretboard’s scale, mode, or chord are shown as chips
at the top of the panel:

Fretboard note and interval chips

Each chip shows a note and its interval within the pattern, using the
following notations:

-   P1 — Perfect unison (root)

-   m2 — Minor second

-   M2 — Major second

-   m3 — Minor third

-   M3 — Major third

-   P4 — Perfect fourth

-   d5 — Diminished fifth

-   A4 — Augmented fourth

-   P5 — Perfect fifth

-   A5 — Augmented fifth

-   m6 — Minor sixth

-   M6 — Major sixth

-   d7 — Diminished seventh

-   m7 — Minor seventh

-   M7 — Major seventh

-   P8 — Perfect octave

-   m9 — Minor ninth

-   M9 — Major ninth

-   m10 — Minor tenth

-   M10 — Major tenth

-   P11 — Perfect eleventh

-   A11 — Augmented eleventh

-   P12 — Perfect twelfth

-   m13 — Minor thirteenth

-   M13 — Major thirteenth

Hovering over a chip highlights that note or interval across the
fretboard.

Clicking a chip toggles that note or interval between visible and hidden
across the fretboard:

Example of hidden notes

Note: all notes can be re-enabled using the reset icon to the right of
the chips.

Note: hidden notes are not played when using “Pattern play”.

-   Fretboard controls – marking To the right of the chips is a bank of
    additional fretboard controls. The first group of three buttons
    relates to marking notes. The notes can be manually marked
    individually, be marked due to their visibility (see note/interval
    chips and string controls) or be marked by the Scale Harmony sheet.
    There is a lot of flexibility and functionality in the marking
    system, and the number of controls has been designed to be minimal,
    rather than have a multitude of similar but confusing controls. But
    it does mean that some experimentation is required in order to work
    out its subtleties and how it might work best for your usage.
    Fretboard controls (top right) You can mark individual notes on a
    fretboard. Marked notes are highlighted with a green dashed circle.
    Frettly supports up to nine marking groups and defaults to using
    group one, indicated to the left of the marking controls. The
    current group can be changed using the up and down controls.
    Clicking the group’s label opens a pop-up sheet that allows the
    label to be edited. To enable marking, click the first button,
    showing the marker pen. When marking is on, the button turns yellow.
    Click any note to toggle its mark. The marker button also shows how
    many notes are marked in the current group, including notes that may
    be off-screen. Fretboard in marking mode While marking is enabled,
    clicking notes only marks or unmarks them. The usual play
    interactions are paused until you turn marking off. Click the marker
    pen again to return to normal fretboard mode. Your chosen document
    play mode then applies as usual, and any marked notes remain visible
    alongside unmarked notes. To mark every occurrence of one or more
    notes across the fretboard, use a combination of hiding notes with
    the note chips at the top of the fretboard and hiding entire strings
    with the left-hand string controls. Then right-click the marker pen
    button and select either “Mark all shown notes” or “Unmark all shown
    notes”. Fretboard marking context menu The second button erases all
    marks within the current marking group. The erasure can be undone
    using Undo (⌘Z). Right-clicking the erase button reveals a context
    menu offering two options. The first erases the current group’s
    markings, and the second erases the markings from all nine groups.
    Fretboard marking erasure context menu The third button is “View
    marked notes”. This toggles the fretboard’s display between marked
    notes only and all notes. This allows you to focus on a particular
    section or sequence of notes on the fretboard. When “View marked
    notes” is enabled, the button is coloured yellow. When it is not
    enabled but the current group contains marks, it has a yellow
    outline to remind you that the group has marks which can be viewed
    in isolation. Fretboard showing only marked notes Scale Harmony
    marked chords The Scale Harmony sheet launched from a fretboard
    offers the option to “Replace Marked Groups” with groups constructed
    from the chords shown in Scale Harmony. For example, it can mark
    every occurrence of the notes C, E, and G for a Cmaj chord, and
    label that group “Cmaj”. When marking groups have been set up in
    this way, their marks cannot be edited: you cannot add or remove
    marks. This ensures that a marking group labelled as representing a
    chord remains accurate. You cannot edit the title of such a group
    either. To reclaim a chord-based marking group, erase it, after
    which it reverts to being an ordinary marking group. When might you
    want to use marked notes and view only marked notes? Perhaps when
    you want to focus on a specific chord fingering, or break down a
    scale into playable positions along the fretboard, as is commonly
    done for pentatonic scales: Viewing marked notes on a fretboard

Fretboard controls – marking

To the right of the chips is a bank of additional fretboard controls.
The first group of three buttons relates to marking notes.

The notes can be manually marked individually, be marked due to their
visibility (see note/interval chips and string controls) or be marked by
the Scale Harmony sheet. There is a lot of flexibility and functionality
in the marking system, and the number of controls has been designed to
be minimal, rather than have a multitude of similar but confusing
controls. But it does mean that some experimentation is required in
order to work out its subtleties and how it might work best for your
usage.

Fretboard controls (top right)

You can mark individual notes on a fretboard. Marked notes are
highlighted with a green dashed circle.

Frettly supports up to nine marking groups and defaults to using group
one, indicated to the left of the marking controls. The current group
can be changed using the up and down controls. Clicking the group’s
label opens a pop-up sheet that allows the label to be edited.

To enable marking, click the first button, showing the marker pen. When
marking is on, the button turns yellow. Click any note to toggle its
mark. The marker button also shows how many notes are marked in the
current group, including notes that may be off-screen.

Fretboard in marking mode

While marking is enabled, clicking notes only marks or unmarks them. The
usual play interactions are paused until you turn marking off. Click the
marker pen again to return to normal fretboard mode. Your chosen
document play mode then applies as usual, and any marked notes remain
visible alongside unmarked notes.

To mark every occurrence of one or more notes across the fretboard, use
a combination of hiding notes with the note chips at the top of the
fretboard and hiding entire strings with the left-hand string controls.
Then right-click the marker pen button and select either “Mark all shown
notes” or “Unmark all shown notes”.

Fretboard marking context menu

The second button erases all marks within the current marking group. The
erasure can be undone using Undo (⌘Z).

Right-clicking the erase button reveals a context menu offering two
options. The first erases the current group’s markings, and the second
erases the markings from all nine groups.

Fretboard marking erasure context menu

The third button is “View marked notes”. This toggles the fretboard’s
display between marked notes only and all notes. This allows you to
focus on a particular section or sequence of notes on the fretboard.

When “View marked notes” is enabled, the button is coloured yellow. When
it is not enabled but the current group contains marks, it has a yellow
outline to remind you that the group has marks which can be viewed in
isolation.

Fretboard showing only marked notes

Scale Harmony marked chords

The Scale Harmony sheet launched from a fretboard offers the option to
“Replace Marked Groups” with groups constructed from the chords shown in
Scale Harmony. For example, it can mark every occurrence of the notes C,
E, and G for a Cmaj chord, and label that group “Cmaj”.

When marking groups have been set up in this way, their marks cannot be
edited: you cannot add or remove marks. This ensures that a marking
group labelled as representing a chord remains accurate. You cannot edit
the title of such a group either. To reclaim a chord-based marking
group, erase it, after which it reverts to being an ordinary marking
group.

When might you want to use marked notes and view only marked notes?
Perhaps when you want to focus on a specific chord fingering, or break
down a scale into playable positions along the fretboard, as is commonly
done for pentatonic scales:

Viewing marked notes on a fretboard

-   Fretboard controls – notepad The fourth button is the notepad
    control. A new fretboard starts with an empty notepad, so the button
    appears plain. Clicking it toggles the notepad’s visibility. Once
    the notepad contains text, the button turns yellow to remind you
    that notes are present. Notepads are saved with their fretboards in
    the Frettly document. Fretboard notepad

Fretboard controls – notepad

The fourth button is the notepad control. A new fretboard starts with an
empty notepad, so the button appears plain. Clicking it toggles the
notepad’s visibility. Once the notepad contains text, the button turns
yellow to remind you that notes are present. Notepads are saved with
their fretboards in the Frettly document.

Fretboard notepad

-   Fretboard controls – labelling mode The fifth button toggles between
    viewing notes and viewing intervals. By default, fretboards show
    note labels, and the tooltip shows the corresponding interval. When
    you switch to interval labels, the tooltip text changes accordingly.
    Fretboard showing note labels Fretboard showing interval labels

Fretboard controls – labelling mode

The fifth button toggles between viewing notes and viewing intervals. By
default, fretboards show note labels, and the tooltip shows the
corresponding interval. When you switch to interval labels, the tooltip
text changes accordingly.

Fretboard showing note labels

Fretboard showing interval labels

Chord organiser panels | Frettly Help

Chord organiser panels overview

Just as a Frettly document can contain many fretboard panels, it can
also contain chord organiser panels mixed with those fretboard panels.
Whereas a fretboard panel is configured to show a specific scale, mode
or chord, and provides a configuration panel to change its nature, chord
organisers are generic containers for chords - they can contain whatever
chords you choose, up to a maximum of twelve.

Just as fretboard panels can be dragged within the same or another
document, so too can chord organiser panels. And just as a fretboard
dragged or copy/pasted into another document, changes to show the new
documents instrument and/or tuning, so too do chord organisers.

-   Organiser configuration Chord organiser panels have an edit config
    button, like fretboard panels, but the only thing that can be
    changed is the panels title. Chord diagrams within an organiser also
    have an edit config button, which opens an edit sheet within which
    the chords root note and pattern can be modified.

Organiser configuration

-   Organiser panel controls Chord organiser panels have two controls on
    their top right, similar to those in fretboard panels: Chord
    organiser controls The first of those controls opens the ‘Add chord’
    dialog, from where you choose the root note and pattern type for the
    new chord: Chord organiser add chord dialog Only twelve chords can
    be added per organiser panel - if more are needed, create another
    chord organiser panel and place chords in that. The chord cards in
    an organiser can be dragged from one organiser to another, and can
    be copied and pasted. Click a chord cards title to give it focus,
    then use ⌘C to copy it. Pasting with ⌘V will place the copy into a
    focused organiser panel, or after whichever chord chip within an
    organiser has the focus. The second control is the notepad button,
    opening an editable area in which text can be added for the panel.
    And like the fretboard panel notepad, it can then be hidden by
    clicking the notepad button a second time.

Organiser panel controls

Chord organiser panels have two controls on their top right, similar to
those in fretboard panels:

Chord organiser controls

The first of those controls opens the ‘Add chord’ dialog, from where you
choose the root note and pattern type for the new chord:

Chord organiser add chord dialog

Only twelve chords can be added per organiser panel - if more are
needed, create another chord organiser panel and place chords in that.
The chord cards in an organiser can be dragged from one organiser to
another, and can be copied and pasted. Click a chord cards title to give
it focus, then use ⌘C to copy it. Pasting with ⌘V will place the copy
into a focused organiser panel, or after whichever chord chip within an
organiser has the focus.

The second control is the notepad button, opening an editable area in
which text can be added for the panel. And like the fretboard panel
notepad, it can then be hidden by clicking the notepad button a second
time.

-   Organiser navigation Clicking the title of a fretboard panel gives
    it the focus, and keyboard up/down navigation moves that focus to
    adjacent panels. That focus can transfer to a chord organiser panel,
    or it can be forced by clicking its title. Once a chord organiser
    has the focus, pressing the right arrow key will move the focus to
    the first chord diagram within it. Pressing the left arrow key
    instead would move the focus to the last chord diagram. Subsequent
    left/right key presses will move the focus as you would expect
    amongst the diagrams. Clicking the diagram or pressing the space key
    gives a preview of the focused chord. The speed of the strum can be
    changed in the Chords tab in menu item “Frettly” → “Settings”.

Organiser navigation

Clicking the title of a fretboard panel gives it the focus, and keyboard
up/down navigation moves that focus to adjacent panels. That focus can
transfer to a chord organiser panel, or it can be forced by clicking its
title.

Once a chord organiser has the focus, pressing the right arrow key will
move the focus to the first chord diagram within it. Pressing the left
arrow key instead would move the focus to the last chord diagram.
Subsequent left/right key presses will move the focus as you would
expect amongst the diagrams.

Clicking the diagram or pressing the space key gives a preview of the
focused chord. The speed of the strum can be changed in the Chords tab
in menu item “Frettly” → “Settings”.

-   Organiser chord diagrams When Frettly calculates the chord voicings
    shown in the diagrams within a chord organiser, it looks at all the
    permutations of the chords notes across the entire fretboard and
    runs each fingering through a complex scoring algorithm, trying to
    determine the merits of each ie would it require too many fingers,
    is the fret span too large, would fingers be too contorted etc as
    well as ranking the permutations by their musical correctness. It
    builds a list sorted by fret and ranked by what it considers to be
    the playability of the voicing. The diagrams will default to showing
    what Frettly considers to be the most appropriate voicing at the top
    of the fretboard, and provides navigation controls within the chord
    diagram to enable selection of alternative voicings. By default it
    will filter out voicings containing inner muted strings, but if you
    want those to be shown, there is an option to include them, within
    the Chords tab in menu item “Frettly” → “Settings”. There are rare
    chords which to be musically faithful would require more frets to be
    fingered than are possible, and for those chords it will fallback to
    selecting voicings which contain what are considered to be the most
    important characteristics. i.e. it will sacrifice notes for
    intervals which are considered to be less important than others.
    When it does this it will display a yellow ‘i’ marker next to the
    diagram, and clicking that will show which intervals have been
    sacrificed.

Organiser chord diagrams

When Frettly calculates the chord voicings shown in the diagrams within
a chord organiser, it looks at all the permutations of the chords notes
across the entire fretboard and runs each fingering through a complex
scoring algorithm, trying to determine the merits of each ie would it
require too many fingers, is the fret span too large, would fingers be
too contorted etc as well as ranking the permutations by their musical
correctness. It builds a list sorted by fret and ranked by what it
considers to be the playability of the voicing.

The diagrams will default to showing what Frettly considers to be the
most appropriate voicing at the top of the fretboard, and provides
navigation controls within the chord diagram to enable selection of
alternative voicings.

By default it will filter out voicings containing inner muted strings,
but if you want those to be shown, there is an option to include them,
within the Chords tab in menu item “Frettly” → “Settings”.

There are rare chords which to be musically faithful would require more
frets to be fingered than are possible, and for those chords it will
fallback to selecting voicings which contain what are considered to be
the most important characteristics. i.e. it will sacrifice notes for
intervals which are considered to be less important than others. When it
does this it will display a yellow ‘i’ marker next to the diagram, and
clicking that will show which intervals have been sacrificed.

Editing Essentials in Frettly

Editing Essentials

Some of Frettly’s most useful features are not obvious and are not
visible in its user interface. They are features you are already used to
from applications such as Pages and Numbers, and might not expect to see
in an app like Frettly, so it is worth calling them out and explaining
them here.

-   File Menu Frettly is a document based app like Pages. Just as you
    create documents in Pages for specific purposes, Frettly documents
    can each be tailored for a specific learning exercise or fretboard
    exploration. One document might be for exploring blues scales,
    another for pentatonic explorations. The ‘File’ item in Frettly’s
    menu gives you access to standard macOS file related operations.
    From this menu you may create new Frettly documents, open documents
    etc as well as use the macOS document revision system to revert to a
    previous version of your document.

File Menu

-   Edit Menu When a fretboard panel title area is clicked, that
    fretboard becomes the current/focussed fretboard, and will be the
    fretboard scrolled back to when the “Home” button is clicked. Use
    keyboard up and down arrows to move the current focus. That
    currently focussed fretboard will also be the target for the
    Cut/Copy/Delete options in the Edit menu. A Cut or Delete of a
    fretboard can be undone from the Edit menu using Undo and re-applied
    with Redo. Marking of notes and selection of fretboard
    notes/intervals using the centre top ‘chips’ can also be reverted or
    re-applied using Undo/Redo The Select All menu command will only
    work for text in fretboard noteboards ie it will not select all
    fretboards. The combination of Edit menu commands
    Cut/Copy/Paste/Delete/Undo/Redo provides a powerful way to edit and
    manage fretboards within a document, and also across documents.

Edit Menu

-   Drag And Drop Fretboards, chord organisers (and the chord diagrams
    within them) can be moved within a document by dragging them by
    their drag handle/title area and dropping them in the upper or lower
    half of another panel (which determines whether they will be moved
    to before or after the fretboard they are dropped onto). A blue line
    will appear confirming the drop location. The target fretboard needs
    to be close when dragging like this, so with documents having many
    fretboards, moving fretboards is easier with the “Arrange panels”
    button in the document header.

Drag And Drop

-   Cross Document Editing Fretboards can also be dragged from one
    Frettly document into another. When they are, the original fretboard
    remains in the source document, and a copy is placed in the
    destination document, following the pattern described above
    (upper/lower half of a fretboard determining before/after). If the
    destination document does not have any fretboards yet, simply drop
    into the ‘Welcome to Frettly’ introductory text panel. Copy/Paste
    between documents also works and is a useful way of replicating
    fretboards, even when the two documents are for different
    instruments/tunings - the fretboard added will automatically be
    adjusted to show the same scale/chord but in the context of that
    document’s instrument/tuning. Notepad text is copied across, as is
    the nature of the scale/chord/mode. If the destination document’s
    configuration (instrument/tuning) matches that of the source
    document, then marked notes will also be copied, else they will be
    cleared. ie Frettly will retain what makes sense in the fretboard
    copy within the target document.

Cross Document Editing

-   View Menu The View menu item enables switching tabbed mode on/off.
    When tabbed mode is on, it is possible to open multiple Frettly
    documents within the same window, and switch between tabs using Ctrl
    Tab. Copy/Paste of fretboards works within tabbed mode, just as it
    does within separately windowed documents, but drag and drop cannot.

View Menu

Frettly Settings and Preferences

Frettly Settings

While each fretboard has controls within it which determine the
characteristics of just that fretboard, and each document has document
wide configuration (instrument, tuning and handedness) which apply to
all of the fretboards within it, Frettly has application wide settings,
found in menubar item “Frettly” -> “Settings”.

Settings are divided into tabs: General - Miscellaneous settings
Documents - Settings which affect document level functionality
Instruments - Settings for Frettlys supported instruments Fretboards -
Settings which affect fretboard level functionality Support - Help
related

-   General - Miscellaneous settings

-   Documents - Settings which affect document level functionality

-   Instruments - Settings for Frettlys supported instruments

-   Fretboards - Settings which affect fretboard level functionality

-   Support - Help related

-   General On first installation, Frettly presents an introductory
    welcome page, offering to create a new document, open an existing
    one, or open the inbuilt example document. That welcome page can be
    set to not re-appear on future application launch, but should you
    wish to have it re-appear, the option is here. Frettly Settings -
    General

General

-   On first installation, Frettly presents an introductory welcome
    page, offering to create a new document, open an existing one, or
    open the inbuilt example document. That welcome page can be set to
    not re-appear on future application launch, but should you wish to
    have it re-appear, the option is here.

Frettly Settings - General

-   Documents When creating a new document, you are prompted for
    instrument, tuning and handedness , with the latter defaulting to
    right-handed, favouring the majority of players. If you are in the
    minority, set the default to be left-handed here. The play mode when
    clicking fretboard notes has three settings, sustained, single or
    pattern run. The default mode for new documents can be set in this
    tab. The play mode can be changed within the document at any time
    once it has at least one fretboard. Frettly Settings - Documents

Documents

-   When creating a new document, you are prompted for instrument,
    tuning and handedness , with the latter defaulting to right-handed,
    favouring the majority of players. If you are in the minority, set
    the default to be left-handed here.

-   The play mode when clicking fretboard notes has three settings,
    sustained, single or pattern run. The default mode for new documents
    can be set in this tab. The play mode can be changed within the
    document at any time once it has at least one fretboard.

Frettly Settings - Documents

-   Instruments This section gives you control over the default maximum
    number of frets Frettly uses for each instrument type. That means
    fretboards can better reflect the instruments you actually play,
    whether your guitar stops at 22 frets, your mandolin has a shorter
    fingerboard, or your ukulele has a more compact range. Use the
    sliders to choose the fret limit that best matches each of your own
    instruments. Frettly will then use those values whenever it display
    a fretboard in a document of that instrument type. Sliders shown in
    grey indicate instruments using Frettly’s built-in defaults, while
    blue sliders indicate an instrument is using a default override.
    Frettly Settings - Instruments

Instruments

-   This section gives you control over the default maximum number of
    frets Frettly uses for each instrument type. That means fretboards
    can better reflect the instruments you actually play, whether your
    guitar stops at 22 frets, your mandolin has a shorter fingerboard,
    or your ukulele has a more compact range.

-   Use the sliders to choose the fret limit that best matches each of
    your own instruments. Frettly will then use those values whenever it
    display a fretboard in a document of that instrument type. Sliders
    shown in grey indicate instruments using Frettly’s built-in
    defaults, while blue sliders indicate an instrument is using a
    default override.

Frettly Settings - Instruments

-   Fretboards Background - choose here which wood graphic you would
    like for all fretboards in all documents. The choice you make is
    seen immediately in any open documents, so you can see the effect
    straight away if you have open documents, but a preview is also
    shown in the dialog. Note colours - depending on the background you
    choose, you may like to choose darker/lighter or subdued/vibrant
    colours for the notes and intervals shown in fretboards. Again, your
    selection is instantly applied to open documents and a preview is
    provided in the settings dialog. The two Mono themes provide
    monochrome colouring for those who prefer uniform note colouring.
    Frettly Settings - Fretboards

Fretboards

-   Background - choose here which wood graphic you would like for all
    fretboards in all documents. The choice you make is seen immediately
    in any open documents, so you can see the effect straight away if
    you have open documents, but a preview is also shown in the dialog.

-   Note colours - depending on the background you choose, you may like
    to choose darker/lighter or subdued/vibrant colours for the notes
    and intervals shown in fretboards. Again, your selection is
    instantly applied to open documents and a preview is provided in the
    settings dialog. The two Mono themes provide monochrome colouring
    for those who prefer uniform note colouring.

Frettly Settings - Fretboards

-   Chords Voicing depth - when Frettly calculates the chord voicings
    for diagrams in chord organisers, it calculates a number of options
    at each fret position. Those options are then ranked based on how
    likely they are to be playable, and the navigation controls within
    the diagram allow you to cycle through the alternative voicings. The
    number of alternatives is controlled by the voicing depth here. The
    higher the number, the more alternatives you may get, but the longer
    it will take to navigate through them. Strum speed - when you press
    the space key with a chord voicing diagram selected, or if you click
    it, Frettly will play that voicing, string by string as a strum. The
    speed of that strum is set here. Always right handed - the chord
    voicing diagrams will by default display their strings using the
    orientation of the document instruments handedness. So if the
    instrument is right handed, string six will be on the far left, but
    if left handed, it will be on the right. If as a left handed player
    you prefer to see these chord diagrams in a right handed
    orientation, set the option here. Include muted interior strings -
    Frettly will filter out chord voicings having interior muted
    strings, regarding them as not ideal. If you want to see those
    alternative voicings, set this option, but depending on the voicing
    depth above, voicings with no interior muted strings will be pushed
    down the ranking and possibly sacrificed. Frettly Settings - Chords

Chords

-   Voicing depth - when Frettly calculates the chord voicings for
    diagrams in chord organisers, it calculates a number of options at
    each fret position. Those options are then ranked based on how
    likely they are to be playable, and the navigation controls within
    the diagram allow you to cycle through the alternative voicings. The
    number of alternatives is controlled by the voicing depth here. The
    higher the number, the more alternatives you may get, but the longer
    it will take to navigate through them.

-   Strum speed - when you press the space key with a chord voicing
    diagram selected, or if you click it, Frettly will play that
    voicing, string by string as a strum. The speed of that strum is set
    here.

-   Always right handed - the chord voicing diagrams will by default
    display their strings using the orientation of the document
    instruments handedness. So if the instrument is right handed, string
    six will be on the far left, but if left handed, it will be on the
    right. If as a left handed player you prefer to see these chord
    diagrams in a right handed orientation, set the option here.

-   Include muted interior strings - Frettly will filter out chord
    voicings having interior muted strings, regarding them as not ideal.
    If you want to see those alternative voicings, set this option, but
    depending on the voicing depth above, voicings with no interior
    muted strings will be pushed down the ranking and possibly
    sacrificed.

Frettly Settings - Chords

-   Support A link to this website is provided within this tab. Frettly
    Settings - Support

Support

-   A link to this website is provided within this tab.

Frettly Settings - Support

Frettly – Privacy

Privacy

frettly.app is a simple informational site. It does not use custom analytics or tracking.

Overview

This page explains what data may be processed when you visit
frettly.app, and what happens if you choose to contact us by email.

Last updated: 26th January 2026 Controller / contact: Data Controller —
data.controller@frettly.app

Frettly (the app)

Overview

Frettly is designed to work without collecting personal information.
Your fretboards/documents are stored locally on your Mac (and only sync
if you choose to store them in a cloud location such as iCloud Drive via
Finder/Files).

Data the app collects

-   No personal data is collected by the app.

-   The app does not include advertising, third-party analytics, or
    tracking.

In-App Purchases

If you purchase Frettly Pro, the transaction is processed by Apple. We
do not receive your payment details. Apple may associate purchases with
your Apple ID according to Apple’s policies.

Support

If you contact us for support, we will receive your email address and
the contents of your message. We use that information only to respond
and improve Frettly.

frettly.app (this site)

We do not intentionally collect personal data through this website and
we do not run our own analytics. However, like most websites, some
technical data may be processed to deliver pages reliably and securely.

Basic technical data

-   Your IP address (or a portion of it), browser type, device details,
    and request metadata may appear in access logs.

-   This information is typically used for security, performance, and
    troubleshooting (for example, protecting the site from abuse).

Hosting and delivery providers

frettly.app is hosted using GitHub Pages and delivered via Cloudflare
(DNS/CDN). These providers may process technical data (including IP
address and request information) when you access the site.

Cookies

frettly.app does not intentionally set non-essential cookies for
advertising or analytics. However, GitHub and/or Cloudflare may set
cookies or similar technologies that are strictly necessary for site
delivery and security.

Email contact

If you email us with feedback, feature requests, or support questions,
we will receive your email address and the contents of your message.

-   We use this information only to respond and to improve Frettly.

-   We do not sell your email address or message content.

Data retention

-   Emails are kept only as long as needed to respond and to maintain
    reasonable support history.

-   Website technical logs are retained according to the policies of the
    hosting/CDN providers (GitHub / Cloudflare).

Sharing

We do not share personal data with third parties except where required
to operate the site (GitHub Pages and Cloudflare), or where required by
law.

Your rights

Depending on your location (for example, under UK GDPR), you may have
rights to request access to, correction of, or deletion of personal data
we hold about you (such as support emails). To make a request, contact:

Changes to this policy

We may update this privacy notice from time to time. The “Last updated”
date at the top of this page will reflect the most recent change.
